Bone Marrow Mesenchymal Stem-Cell Transplantation Promotes Functional Improvement Associated with CNTF-STAT3 Activation after Hemi-Sectioned Spinal Cord Injury in Tree Shrews

evidence_entities.text: Hemi-sectioned spinal cord injury (hSCI) can lead to spastic paralysis on the injured side, as well as flaccid paralysis on the contralateral side
Stem-cell therapy may offer an effective treatment option for individuals with hSCI

Following BMSC transplantation, motoneurons extent of shrinkage were reduced and the animals' Basso, Beattie, and Bresnahan (BBB) locomotion scale scores were significantly higher at 21 and 28 days after injury
Corresponding elevations in the expression of STAT3 and CNTF mRNA were observed in the hSCI + BMSCs group
BMSC transplantation improved motor function in tree shrews subjected to hSCI by increasing the CNTF-regulated STAT3 signaling

properties.note: Same condition described with different terminology

notes: This study demonstrates the therapeutic potential of bone marrow mesenchymal stem cell transplantation for spinal cord injury through the CNTF-STAT3 signaling pathway
Tree shrews were used as an animal model that more closely resembles primates than rodents
The study shows functional improvement measured by BBB locomotion scale scores at 21 and 28 days post-injury
